Description: Traditional Model-View-Controller with Controller → Service → Repository layers. Clear separation of concerns with each layer having a single responsibility.
Structure:
src/
  controllers/
  services/
  repositories/
  models/
  middleware/
  routes/
  config/
When To Use: Small to medium REST APIs; CRUD-heavy applications; teams familiar with MVC pattern
When Not To Use: Complex domain logic requiring DDD; event-driven architectures; microservices
Key Principles: Single responsibility per layer; dependencies flow inward; controllers handle HTTP only; services contain business logic; repositories handle data access

Description: Dependency flows inward: Entities → Use Cases → Interface Adapters → Frameworks. Core business logic has zero external dependencies.
Structure:
src/
  domain/
    entities/
    use-cases/
  infrastructure/
    database/
    http/
  interfaces/
    controllers/
    presenters/
When To Use: Complex business logic; long-lived projects; multiple delivery mechanisms (REST + GraphQL + CLI)
When Not To Use: Simple CRUD apps; prototypes; small teams where overhead isn't justified
Key Principles: Dependency Rule (inner layers don't know outer); Use Cases orchestrate entities; Infrastructure is pluggable; Test core without frameworks

Description: Application core defines ports (interfaces). External systems connect via adapters. NestJS modules naturally support this with providers and injection tokens.
Structure:
src/
  modules/
    user/
      domain/
      ports/
      adapters/
      user.module.ts
When To Use: NestJS enterprise apps; when external services change frequently; high testability requirements
When Not To Use: Simple scripts; small APIs; when team is unfamiliar with the pattern
Key Principles: Ports are interfaces defined by the core; Adapters implement ports; Core is framework-agnostic; Easy to swap implementations

Description: Organize code by feature/domain modules within a single deployable unit. Each module owns its routes, services, and data access. Modules communicate via well-defined interfaces.
Structure:
src/
  modules/
    auth/
      auth.controller.ts
      auth.service.ts
      auth.repository.ts
      auth.schema.ts
    product/
    order/
  shared/
    middleware/
    lib/
When To Use: Medium to large apps; teams working on separate features; preparing for potential microservices split
When Not To Use: Tiny apps with 1-2 entities; when modules have heavy cross-dependencies
Key Principles: Module independence; shared nothing between modules; each module owns its database tables; clear public API per module

Description: Independently deployable services communicating via HTTP/gRPC/message queues. Each service owns its data store and can be scaled independently.
Structure:
services/
  user-service/
  order-service/
  payment-service/
  gateway/
libs/
  shared-types/
  common/
When To Use: Large teams (>10 devs); independent scaling requirements; polyglot persistence needs; high availability
When Not To Use: Small teams; simple apps; when distributed complexity isn't justified; tight budget
Key Principles: Service independence; API gateway pattern; eventual consistency; circuit breakers; each service owns its data

Description: Individual functions deployed to cloud platforms. No server management. Pay per execution. Ideal for event-driven workloads.
Structure:
functions/
  api/
    users.ts
    orders.ts
  events/
    process-payment.ts
  lib/
    db.ts
    auth.ts
When To Use: Event-driven workloads; variable traffic; cost optimization for low-traffic APIs; webhook handlers
When Not To Use: Long-running processes; WebSocket-heavy apps; low-latency requirements
Key Principles: Stateless functions; cold start awareness; minimize bundle size; use connection pooling; idempotent handlers

Description: Multiple packages/apps in a single repository with shared dependencies and build caching. Turborepo provides incremental builds and task orchestration.
Structure:
apps/
  web/
  api/
  mobile/
packages/
  ui/
  config/
  tsconfig/
  shared-types/
turbo.json
When To Use: Multiple related apps; shared component libraries; consistent tooling across projects; large organizations
When Not To Use: Single small app; unrelated projects; when teams need full autonomy
Key Principles: Shared dependencies; incremental builds; consistent versioning; task caching; workspace protocols

Description: Components communicate through events rather than direct calls. Enables loose coupling and async processing. Can be in-process (EventEmitter) or distributed (Redis/RabbitMQ).
Structure:
src/
  events/
    handlers/
    publishers/
    subscribers/
  domain/
  infrastructure/
    message-bus/
When To Use: Decoupled components; audit trails; async processing; real-time features; when actions trigger multiple side effects
When Not To Use: Simple request-response APIs; when event ordering is critical and hard to guarantee; small apps
Key Principles: Events are immutable facts; handlers are idempotent; eventual consistency; event store for replay; dead letter queues

Description: Separate read and write models. Commands mutate state, queries return data. Enables independent optimization of read and write paths.
Structure:
src/
  commands/
    create-order.command.ts
    create-order.handler.ts
  queries/
    get-orders.query.ts
    get-orders.handler.ts
  models/
    write/
    read/
When To Use: High-performance read/write split; complex domain with different read/write patterns; event sourcing companion
When Not To Use: Simple CRUD; when read and write models are identical; small teams
Key Principles: Commands return void or ID; Queries are side-effect free; Read models optimized for queries; Write models enforce invariants

Description: Architectural methodology for frontend apps. Layers: app → pages → widgets → features → entities → shared. Each layer can only import from layers below.
Structure:
src/
  app/
  pages/
  widgets/
  features/
    auth/
    cart/
  entities/
    user/
    product/
  shared/
    ui/
    lib/
    api/
When To Use: Large frontend applications; Next.js/React apps with complex features; teams needing clear boundaries
When Not To Use: Small apps; backend-only projects; when team prefers simpler flat structure
Key Principles: Unidirectional imports (top to bottom only); public API per slice; isolation between slices; shared layer for reusable primitives

Description: Full-stack architecture using Next.js App Router with React Server Components for data fetching and Server Actions for mutations.
Structure:
app/
  (auth)/
    login/page.tsx
  dashboard/
    layout.tsx
    page.tsx
  api/
    [...route]/route.ts
lib/
  db.ts
  auth.ts
components/
  ui/
When To Use: Full-stack React apps; SEO-important sites; content-heavy applications; when server rendering is beneficial
When Not To Use: Pure API backends; non-React frontends; when you need WebSocket support; real-time heavy apps
Key Principles: Server Components by default; Client Components only for interactivity; Server Actions for mutations; Route Handlers for API endpoints

Description: Full-stack web framework based on web standards. Loaders fetch data, Actions handle mutations. Progressive enhancement with HTML forms.
Structure:
app/
  routes/
    _index.tsx
    dashboard.tsx
    dashboard.users.tsx
  models/
    user.server.ts
  utils/
    session.server.ts
  components/
When To Use: Form-heavy applications; progressive enhancement needs; when web standards matter; content sites
When Not To Use: Heavy SPA interactions; real-time apps; when you need extensive client-side state
Key Principles: Loaders for data; Actions for mutations; Progressive enhancement; Nested routes; .server suffix for server-only code

Description: Core system with extension points. Plugins register handlers/middleware via a plugin registry. Fastify uses this pattern natively.
Structure:
src/
  core/
    plugin-registry.ts
    hooks.ts
  plugins/
    auth/
    logging/
    metrics/
  app.ts
When To Use: Extensible systems; when third-party extensions are needed; Fastify apps; build systems
When Not To Use: Simple apps; when all features are known upfront; tight coupling is acceptable
Key Principles: Plugin isolation; lifecycle hooks; dependency ordering; graceful degradation if plugin fails

Description: Single entry point that routes requests to internal services. Handles cross-cutting concerns: auth, rate limiting, request aggregation.
Structure:
gateway/
  src/
    routes/
    middleware/
      auth.ts
      rate-limit.ts
    proxy/
    aggregation/
When To Use: Microservice architectures; BFF (Backend For Frontend); when clients need unified API; cross-cutting concerns
When Not To Use: Monolithic apps; single service; when gateway becomes a bottleneck
Key Principles: Thin gateway (no business logic); consistent auth/logging; request aggregation for reducing round trips; circuit breaker for downstream services

Description: Each feature is a self-contained module with its own controller, service, repository, and tests colocated together.
Structure:
src/
  modules/
    user/
      user.controller.ts
      user.service.ts
      user.repository.ts
      user.schema.ts
      user.test.ts
      index.ts
    product/
    order/
When To Use: Medium to large apps; when features are relatively independent; team-per-feature organization
When Not To Use: Tiny apps; when features share heavy logic; when horizontal layers are clearer
Key Principles: Feature isolation; colocated tests; barrel export per module; shared utilities in /shared; minimal cross-module imports

Description: Methodology for building scalable SaaS apps. Config in env vars, stateless processes, explicit dependencies, port binding.
Structure:
src/
  config/
    env.ts (Zod-validated)
  index.ts
Dockerfile
.env.example
When To Use: Production applications; containerized deployments; cloud-native apps
When Not To Use: Quick prototypes; desktop applications; embedded systems
Key Principles: Config via env vars (validated with Zod); explicit deps in package.json; stateless processes; logs as event streams; dev/prod parity

Description: Proper process lifecycle management: handle signals, drain connections, close database pools, finish in-flight requests before exit.
When To Use: All production Node.js servers; containerized apps (Kubernetes); long-running processes
When Not To Use: One-off scripts; serverless functions (platform handles lifecycle)
Key Principles: Handle SIGTERM + SIGINT; close server first (stop accepting); drain active connections; close DB pools; set timeout for force exit

Description: Expose /health and /ready endpoints for load balancers and orchestrators to check application status.
Structure: GET /health → { status: 'ok', uptime }\nGET /ready → { status: 'ok', db: 'connected', redis: 'connected' }
When To Use: Kubernetes deployments; load-balanced services; any production app behind a health-checking proxy
When Not To Use: Simple scripts; development-only apps
Key Principles: Liveness = process alive; Readiness = can serve traffic; check DB/Redis connections in readiness; return quickly (no heavy queries)

Description: All files in a single directory or minimal nesting. Suitable for small projects, scripts, and prototypes.
Structure:
src/
  index.ts
  routes.ts
  db.ts
  auth.ts
  types.ts
When To Use: Small apps; CLI scripts; prototypes; when project has < 10 files
When Not To Use: Growing apps (>15 files); team projects; when navigation becomes difficult
Key Principles: Start flat, refactor when it hurts; no premature abstraction; keep it simple

Description: Model complex business domains with rich domain objects. Aggregates enforce invariants. Repositories abstract persistence.
Structure:
src/
  domain/
    user/
      user.entity.ts
      user.value-objects.ts
      user.repository.ts (interface)
      user.service.ts
    order/
      order.aggregate.ts
      order.events.ts
  infrastructure/
    persistence/
    messaging/
  application/
    use-cases/
When To Use: Complex business domains; when domain experts are available; enterprise applications; when behavior > CRUD
When Not To Use: Simple CRUD apps; when domain is trivial; small prototypes; when team lacks DDD experience
Key Principles: Ubiquitous language; bounded contexts; aggregates as consistency boundaries; domain events; anti-corruption layers
